title: Cryptococcal Meningitis

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Raised intracranial pressure → brain herniation**  
  *Single finding:* Papilloedema OR new focal neurology (6th nerve palsy, limb weakness) → **do NOT perform lumbar puncture without CT head first**; call neurosurgery for urgent ICP control.
- **Cryptococcoma with mass effect** (MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion) → **can mimic toxoplasma or lymphoma**; steroids may worsen fungal infection – **only give antioedema treatment (mannitol or neurosurgery) if confirmed on imaging; avoid dexamethasone**.
- **Bacterial meningitis co-infection** → treat empirically with antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) until CSF culture and CrAg result are back; **don't delay antibiotics if septic or purpuric rash**.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Subacute headache (days–weeks), fever, malaise, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status (confusion, drowsiness)
- **Key exam findings:** Meningism (Kernig’s sign, Brudzinski’s sign), papilloedema (40%), cranial nerve palsies (especially VI), cognitive slowing; in advanced cases – coma, seizures
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Bacterial meningitis** → acute onset, high fever, purpuric rash, neutrophilic CSF with low glucose
- **Tuberculous meningitis** → subacute onset, lower cranial nerve palsies, CSF lymphocytosis, low glucose, high protein; *splitter:* **CSF CrAg positive only in cryptococcus**
- **Viral meningitis** → typically milder, CSF lymphocytic with normal glucose, no cryptococcal antigen
- **Toxoplasma encephalitis** (HIV) → focal neurological signs, multiple ring-enhancing lesions on MRI; *splitter:* **CSF CrAg positive = cryptococcus, not toxoplasma**
- **Primary CNS lymphoma** (HIV) → ring-enhancing lesion, but usually solitary, and CSF EBV PCR positive; CrAg negative
- **Splitter:** “**Serum or CSF cryptococcal antigen test** – positive rules in cryptococcal meningitis, negative rules it out (sensitivity >95%).”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):** Fundoscopy (papilloedema), neurological exam (GCS, pupils, cranial nerves), vital signs (NEWS2)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, HIV test (if unknown), CD4 count (if HIV+), serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First: **CT head** (before LP if: focal signs, papilloedema, GCS <15, seizure, HIV+ with possible mass)  
  - Second: **MRI brain with contrast** if CT normal but suspicion of cryptococcoma or other intracranial lesion
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture – **measure opening pressure** (normal ≤20 cm H₂O) – send CSF for: CrAg, India ink (less sensitive), fungal culture, cell count, protein, glucose  
  - **CSF CrAg** – best initial test; **culture** is gold standard for confirmation (takes 5–7 days)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture with opening pressure** – missing this is a critical error; raised ICP >25 cm H₂O requires serial LP for drainage and affects management
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If raised ICP suspected → CT head first; if mass effect or papilloedema → neurosurgery consult for CSF diversion (external ventricular drain)  
  - If no contraindication → urgent LP with opening pressure  
  - Start empiric antifungal therapy **only if clinical suspicion is high** (e.g. HIV+ with CD4 <100 and positive serum CrAg) – do not wait for CSF result if severely unwell (e.g. GCS <15)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Induction therapy: liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine (see doses below) – monitor renal function, potassium, magnesium daily  
  - Repeat LP daily if opening pressure >25 cm H₂O or symptomatic – drain 20–30 mL to reduce pressure to <20 cm H₂O (or halve pressure)  
  - If ICP refractory → neurosurgery for shunt  
  - In HIV: start antiretroviral therapy **2–4 weeks** after starting antifungal therapy (earlier if ART-naïve with severe disease? – [check local ID policy] – risk of IRIS)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Consolidation: fluconazole for 8 weeks  
  - Secondary prophylaxis: fluconazole until CD4 >100 cells/µL and HIV viral load suppressed for ≥3 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: maintenance IV fluids (0.9% saline at 1–2 mL/kg/h) – avoid overhydration (amphotericin nephrotoxicity)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (unless risk of ICH) – consult neurosurgery  
  - Diet: NBM until swallowing assessed (risk of aspiration due to confusion/raised ICP)  
  - Observations: GCS & NEWS2 1–2 hourly initially, then 4 hourly if stable; strict input/output chart
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line induction (HIV+ or immunocompromised):**  
  - Liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg IV once daily (e.g. 3 mg/kg for first dose, then adjust per local protocol)  
  - + Flucytosine 25 mg/kg orally or IV *qds* (max 100 mg/kg/day; adjust for renal impairment – CrCl <50 mL/min reduce dose)
- **Alternative induction (if flucytosine unavailable):**  
  - Liposomal amphotericin B alone (higher risk of treatment failure) or fluconazole high-dose 800 mg IV daily (reserved for resource-limited settings)
- **Consolidation:** Fluconazole 400–800 mg orally once daily for 8 weeks
- **Secondary prophylaxis (chronic suppression):** Fluconazole 200 mg orally once daily
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Amphotericin B: nephrotoxic – avoid with other nephrotoxins (gentamicin, NSAIDs, contrast); check baseline renal function; pre-hydrate with 500 mL 0.9% saline before dose  
  - Flucytosine: myelosuppression (check FBC weekly); renal adjustment required  
  - Fluconazole: CYP3A4 interactions (warfarin, statins, oral hypoglycaemics) – monitor INR/add-on doses
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not monitoring serum K⁺ and Mg²⁺ during amphotericin B therapy** – hypokalaemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias. Also: **prescribing flucytosine without renal function check** – toxicity in renal impairment.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Raised ICP → blindness, cerebral herniation, death  
  - Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in HIV – new/worsening symptoms 2–6 weeks after starting ART  
  - Drug toxicity: amphotericin (renal, electrolyte, infusion reactions, marrow); flucytosine (neutropenia, diarrhoea)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Hydrocephalus → VP shunt required  
  - Relapse (if induction incomplete or secondary prophylaxis missed)  
  - Persistent visual/hearing loss, cognitive impairment
sections.Prognosis: - Without treatment – universally fatal. With appropriate therapy: mortality 10–25% in UK. Poor prognostic factors: altered consciousness, raised opening pressure >25 cm H₂O, low CSF glucose (<2.2 mmol/L), CD4 <50 cells/µL.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always measure opening pressure on every LP** – it guides therapy and prevents herniation. If >25 cm H₂O, drain enough to normalise.
- **Involve microbiology/ID early** – they help select regimen and monitor drug levels (flucytosine levels if toxicity suspected).
- **Delay ART in HIV patients** – start 2–4 weeks after antifungal initiation to reduce IRIS risk; but for those with severe immunosuppression, consider earlier start per local protocol.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not treat raised ICP with dexamethasone** – it does not improve outcome and may worsen fungal proliferation.
- **Do not start ART at the same time as antifungals** – IRIS can cause life-threatening inflammation.
- **Do not rely on India ink alone for CSF diagnosis** – it is insensitive; always send CSF CrAg and culture.
sections.Safety Netting (If patient goes home – r:
- **Return if:** New or worsening headache, visual blurring, confusion, seizure, fever, neck stiffness
- **Follow-up:** ID clinic within 1 week; arrange repeat LP if residual symptoms; ensure outpatient fluconazole supply
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ITU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or increasing  
  - GCS drop >2 points  
  - New focal neurological signs or seizures  
  - Opening pressure >30 cm H₂O not controlled with serial LP  
  - Sign of herniation (pupil dilates, Cushing’s triad)  
  - Severe drug reaction (amphotericin infusion reaction with hypotension)  
- **Trigger for neurosurgical referral:** Any patient requiring external ventricular drain or VP shunt for persistently high ICP.

rawMarkdown:
## Cryptococcal Meningitis
**One-linter:** Fungal meningitis in immunocompromised (HIV, transplant). High mortality if raised ICP untreated; requires prompt antifungal therapy + ICP management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Raised intracranial pressure → brain herniation**  
  *Single finding:* Papilloedema OR new focal neurology (6th nerve palsy, limb weakness) → **do NOT perform lumbar puncture without CT head first**; call neurosurgery for urgent ICP control.
- **Cryptococcoma with mass effect** (MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion) → **can mimic toxoplasma or lymphoma**; steroids may worsen fungal infection – **only give antioedema treatment (mannitol or neurosurgery) if confirmed on imaging; avoid dexamethasone**.
- **Bacterial meningitis co-infection** → treat empirically with antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) until CSF culture and CrAg result are back; **don't delay antibiotics if septic or purpuric rash**.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Subacute headache (days–weeks), fever, malaise, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status (confusion, drowsiness)
- **Key exam findings:** Meningism (Kernig’s sign, Brudzinski’s sign), papilloedema (40%), cranial nerve palsies (especially VI), cognitive slowing; in advanced cases – coma, seizures

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Bacterial meningitis** → acute onset, high fever, purpuric rash, neutrophilic CSF with low glucose
- **Tuberculous meningitis** → subacute onset, lower cranial nerve palsies, CSF lymphocytosis, low glucose, high protein; *splitter:* **CSF CrAg positive only in cryptococcus**
- **Viral meningitis** → typically milder, CSF lymphocytic with normal glucose, no cryptococcal antigen
- **Toxoplasma encephalitis** (HIV) → focal neurological signs, multiple ring-enhancing lesions on MRI; *splitter:* **CSF CrAg positive = cryptococcus, not toxoplasma**
- **Primary CNS lymphoma** (HIV) → ring-enhancing lesion, but usually solitary, and CSF EBV PCR positive; CrAg negative
- **Splitter:** “**Serum or CSF cryptococcal antigen test** – positive rules in cryptococcal meningitis, negative rules it out (sensitivity >95%).”

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):** Fundoscopy (papilloedema), neurological exam (GCS, pupils, cranial nerves), vital signs (NEWS2)
- **Bloods:** FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, HIV test (if unknown), CD4 count (if HIV+), serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)
- **Imaging:**  
  - First: **CT head** (before LP if: focal signs, papilloedema, GCS <15, seizure, HIV+ with possible mass)  
  - Second: **MRI brain with contrast** if CT normal but suspicion of cryptococcoma or other intracranial lesion
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture – **measure opening pressure** (normal ≤20 cm H₂O) – send CSF for: CrAg, India ink (less sensitive), fungal culture, cell count, protein, glucose  
  - **CSF CrAg** – best initial test; **culture** is gold standard for confirmation (takes 5–7 days)
- **Never-miss test:** **Lumbar puncture with opening pressure** – missing this is a critical error; raised ICP >25 cm H₂O requires serial LP for drainage and affects management

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If raised ICP suspected → CT head first; if mass effect or papilloedema → neurosurgery consult for CSF diversion (external ventricular drain)  
  - If no contraindication → urgent LP with opening pressure  
  - Start empiric antifungal therapy **only if clinical suspicion is high** (e.g. HIV+ with CD4 <100 and positive serum CrAg) – do not wait for CSF result if severely unwell (e.g. GCS <15)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Induction therapy: liposomal amphotericin B + flucytosine (see doses below) – monitor renal function, potassium, magnesium daily  
  - Repeat LP daily if opening pressure >25 cm H₂O or symptomatic – drain 20–30 mL to reduce pressure to <20 cm H₂O (or halve pressure)  
  - If ICP refractory → neurosurgery for shunt  
  - In HIV: start antiretroviral therapy **2–4 weeks** after starting antifungal therapy (earlier if ART-naïve with severe disease? – [check local ID policy] – risk of IRIS)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Consolidation: fluconazole for 8 weeks  
  - Secondary prophylaxis: fluconazole until CD4 >100 cells/µL and HIV viral load suppressed for ≥3 months
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: maintenance IV fluids (0.9% saline at 1–2 mL/kg/h) – avoid overhydration (amphotericin nephrotoxicity)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (unless risk of ICH) – consult neurosurgery  
  - Diet: NBM until swallowing assessed (risk of aspiration due to confusion/raised ICP)  
  - Observations: GCS & NEWS2 1–2 hourly initially, then 4 hourly if stable; strict input/output chart

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line induction (HIV+ or immunocompromised):**  
  - Liposomal amphotericin B 3–5 mg/kg IV once daily (e.g. 3 mg/kg for first dose, then adjust per local protocol)  
  - + Flucytosine 25 mg/kg orally or IV *qds* (max 100 mg/kg/day; adjust for renal impairment – CrCl <50 mL/min reduce dose)
- **Alternative induction (if flucytosine unavailable):**  
  - Liposomal amphotericin B alone (higher risk of treatment failure) or fluconazole high-dose 800 mg IV daily (reserved for resource-limited settings)
- **Consolidation:** Fluconazole 400–800 mg orally once daily for 8 weeks
- **Secondary prophylaxis (chronic suppression):** Fluconazole 200 mg orally once daily
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Amphotericin B: nephrotoxic – avoid with other nephrotoxins (gentamicin, NSAIDs, contrast); check baseline renal function; pre-hydrate with 500 mL 0.9% saline before dose  
  - Flucytosine: myelosuppression (check FBC weekly); renal adjustment required  
  - Fluconazole: CYP3A4 interactions (warfarin, statins, oral hypoglycaemics) – monitor INR/add-on doses
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not monitoring serum K⁺ and Mg²⁺ during amphotericin B therapy** – hypokalaemia can cause cardiac arrhythmias. Also: **prescribing flucytosine without renal function check** – toxicity in renal impairment.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Raised ICP → blindness, cerebral herniation, death  
  - Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in HIV – new/worsening symptoms 2–6 weeks after starting ART  
  - Drug toxicity: amphotericin (renal, electrolyte, infusion reactions, marrow); flucytosine (neutropenia, diarrhoea)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Hydrocephalus → VP shunt required  
  - Relapse (if induction incomplete or secondary prophylaxis missed)  
  - Persistent visual/hearing loss, cognitive impairment

### Prognosis
- Without treatment – universally fatal. With appropriate therapy: mortality 10–25% in UK. Poor prognostic factors: altered consciousness, raised opening pressure >25 cm H₂O, low CSF glucose (<2.2 mmol/L), CD4 <50 cells/µL.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always measure opening pressure on every LP** – it guides therapy and prevents herniation. If >25 cm H₂O, drain enough to normalise.
- **Involve microbiology/ID early** – they help select regimen and monitor drug levels (flucytosine levels if toxicity suspected).
- **Delay ART in HIV patients** – start 2–4 weeks after antifungal initiation to reduce IRIS risk; but for those with severe immunosuppression, consider earlier start per local protocol.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not treat raised ICP with dexamethasone** – it does not improve outcome and may worsen fungal proliferation.
- **Do not start ART at the same time as antifungals** – IRIS can cause life-threatening inflammation.
- **Do not rely on India ink alone for CSF diagnosis** – it is insensitive; always send CSF CrAg and culture.

### Safety Netting (If patient goes home – rare in acute phase)
- **Return if:** New or worsening headache, visual blurring, confusion, seizure, fever, neck stiffness
- **Follow-up:** ID clinic within 1 week; arrange repeat LP if residual symptoms; ensure outpatient fluconazole supply

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ITU if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or increasing  
  - GCS drop >2 points  
  - New focal neurological signs or seizures  
  - Opening pressure >30 cm H₂O not controlled with serial LP  
  - Sign of herniation (pupil dilates, Cushing’s triad)  
  - Severe drug reaction (amphotericin infusion reaction with hypotension)  
- **Trigger for neurosurgical referral:** Any patient requiring external ventricular drain or VP shunt for persistently high ICP.
